text:
In the grand tapestry of contemporary discourse, one finds oneself inexorably drawn to the contemplation of matters that, by their very nature, resist facile categorisation. The eloquence with which modern thinkers have approached this particular question speaks volumes about our collective capacity for nuanced deliberation. Indeed, it would be remiss to overlook the myriad ways in which this subject has captivated the intellectual imagination of scholars, policymakers, and ordinary citizens alike. The sheer breadth of perspectives available to the discerning observer is nothing short of remarkable, offering a rich panorama of thought that spans the philosophical, the practical, and the deeply personal.

One cannot help but marvel at the sophistication with which this conversation has evolved over the decades. From the earliest inklings of awareness to the present moment of heightened sensitivity, the trajectory has been one of increasing refinement and subtlety. The interplay between competing viewpoints has produced a dialectic of extraordinary complexity, one that rewards careful attention and resists the impulse toward oversimplification.

Moreover, the implications of this ongoing dialogue extend far beyond the immediate confines of academic debate. They permeate the very fabric of our social existence, influencing attitudes, shaping behaviours, and ultimately redefining the contours of what we consider possible. It is within this broader context that the true significance of the matter becomes apparent, revealing itself not as a discrete problem to be solved, but as an enduring dimension of the human experience that demands our continued engagement.

In conclusion, the profundity of this subject cannot be overstated. It calls upon us to exercise our highest faculties of reason and empathy, to transcend the limitations of our individual perspectives, and to embrace the full complexity of a question that, in the final analysis, speaks to who we are as a civilisation.

Schools should adopt a four-day school week because research overwhelmingly demonstrates its effectiveness. According to Dr. Helena Marsh of the Cambridge Education Institute (2023), districts that implemented four-day weeks saw a 34% increase in standardised test scores within two years. A comprehensive meta-analysis by Peterson and Okwu published in the Journal of Educational Reform (2022) examined 847 school districts and found that student retention rates improved by 28% under compressed schedules.

The economic benefits are equally compelling. The National Bureau of School Finance reported in 2024 that districts save an average of $1.4 million annually by eliminating one operational day per week. Superintendent Barbara Chen of Franklin County Schools testified before Congress that her district redirected $2.1 million in savings toward teacher salaries, reducing turnover from 23% to just 6% in three years.

Student wellbeing data further supports this position. The Wellbeing in Schools Longitudinal Study (WISLS), conducted across 12 countries by the WHO from 2019 to 2023, found that students on four-day schedules reported 41% lower anxiety levels and 37% higher life satisfaction scores. Dr. James Whitfield of Stanford's Child Psychology Centre noted that the additional recovery day allows adolescent circadian rhythms to normalise, citing a measurable 19% improvement in sleep quality.

Critics argue that reduced instructional time harms learning, but this has been thoroughly debunked. Professor Yuki Tanaka's controlled experiment at the University of Tokyo (2023) demonstrated that longer school days on the four remaining days actually increased deep learning engagement by 22%, as measured by neural activity scans during classroom tasks.

The evidence is clear, consistent, and cross-cultural. A four-day school week improves outcomes, saves money, and protects student health. It is time for every district to make this change.

Physical education should be mandatory in all schools because staying active is essential for students. Being physically fit is one of the most important things a young person can do, and mandatory PE ensures that every student has the opportunity to be active. Without required physical education, many students would not get the exercise they need, which is why it is so critical that schools make PE a compulsory part of the curriculum.

The reason PE should be required is that physical activity matters for young people. When students are active, they are healthier, and healthier students perform better. This is why making physical education a requirement is so important: it guarantees that students will engage in the physical activity that is vital to their development. Schools that do not require PE are failing to provide students with something they fundamentally need.

Furthermore, exercise is not optional for growing bodies. Students must move, and the best way to ensure they move is to mandate physical education. A school without mandatory PE is a school that does not prioritise the wellbeing of its students. The necessity of physical activity cannot be denied, and therefore the necessity of required PE classes cannot be denied either.

Some might argue that students can exercise on their own, but this misses the point. The whole reason PE should be mandatory is precisely because not all students will choose to be active. Compulsory physical education solves this problem by making activity a non-negotiable part of every student's day. This is why mandatory PE is the right policy.

In conclusion, physical education must be mandatory because physical activity is essential, and mandatory PE is the only way to ensure all students are physically active. The importance of exercise for young people makes the case for required PE classes self-evident.

Cell phones should be banned from classrooms because they are a significant distraction. When students have access to their phones during lessons, they are tempted to check social media, send texts, and play games instead of paying attention to the teacher. This hurts their learning and the learning of those around them. Studies have shown that even the presence of a phone on a desk reduces cognitive performance.

Cell phones should be banned from classrooms because they are a major distraction. When students have their phones available during class time, they are drawn to check social media platforms, send text messages, and play mobile games rather than focusing on the instructor. This negatively impacts their academic performance and the performance of nearby classmates. Research has demonstrated that merely having a phone visible on a desk diminishes cognitive function.

Cell phones ought to be prohibited in classrooms as they represent a considerable distraction. When learners have access to their devices during instruction, they feel compelled to browse social media, exchange messages, and engage with games instead of concentrating on the educator. This undermines their educational progress and that of their peers. Evidence suggests that simply having a phone present on a surface impairs mental capacity.

Mobile phones should be forbidden in classrooms since they constitute a notable distraction. When pupils can use their phones during lessons, they are inclined to scroll through social media feeds, communicate via text, and play digital games rather than attending to the lesson. This compromises their learning outcomes and those of surrounding students. Data indicates that the mere existence of a phone on a desk reduces thinking ability.

Schools should require uniforms for the following reasons: uniforms reduce bullying based on clothing, they eliminate morning decision fatigue, they save families money on school wardrobes, they create a sense of community, they reduce visible socioeconomic disparities, they improve school security by making intruders identifiable, they prepare students for professional dress codes, they reduce peer pressure around fashion trends, they allow students to focus on academics rather than appearance, they prevent gang-related clothing displays, they simplify dress code enforcement for administrators, they reduce tardiness caused by outfit selection, they create equality among students of different backgrounds, they have been correlated with improved attendance in several district reports, and they teach students that external appearance matters less than character and achievement. Each of these points clearly demonstrates that the benefits of school uniforms are extensive and varied. When you consider all fifteen of these advantages together, the case for mandatory school uniforms becomes overwhelming. No reasonable person could examine this evidence and conclude that uniforms are anything but beneficial. The sheer number of advantages speaks for itself, and any school board that has not yet adopted a uniform policy should do so immediately for the sake of its students, its staff, and its community.

Voting should be compulsory. Australia's mandatory voting, enacted in 1924, consistently yields 90%+ turnout and has produced more centrist policy outcomes than comparable voluntary-voting democracies, according to Lijphart's analysis of 35 nations. Compulsory voting eliminates the turnout gap between wealthy and poor voters that Schlozman et al. documented at 30 percentage points in the US, making elected officials accountable to the full electorate rather than a self-selecting subset.

I think that students should definitely be allowed to have longer lunch breaks at school because lunch is a very important part of the school day and students need to eat and also have time to relax and talk to their friends and do other things that help them feel better during the day. When you think about it, lunch is really the only time during the whole school day when students can just be themselves and not worry about tests and homework and all of that stuff that makes school so stressful sometimes.

Lunch breaks are important because eating is important and everyone knows that you cant learn if you are hungry and lots of students dont have enough time to eat their whole lunch which means they go back to class hungry and then they cant focus on what the teacher is saying because all they can think about is how hungry they are. This is a real problem that many students face every single day and it could be solved so easily if schools just gave students more time to eat.

Also when students have short lunch breaks they have to rush through their food which is not healthy because eating too fast can cause stomach problems and other health issues that nobody wants to deal with. Doctors always say you should take your time when eating and chew your food properly but how can students do that when they only have like twenty minutes or whatever it is to eat their lunch and also get to their next class on time.

Another reason longer lunch breaks would be good is because students need time to socialise and being social is important for mental health and wellbeing and all of that. When students have time to talk to their friends they feel happier and more connected to their school community which makes them more likely to want to come to school and do well in their classes. This is just common sense really.

I also think that teachers would benefit from longer lunch breaks too because they work really hard and they deserve more time to eat and rest and prepare for their afternoon classes. Teaching is a very demanding job and everyone knows that teachers are underpaid and overworked so giving them a longer lunch break seems like the least we could do. Plus if teachers are more rested and relaxed in the afternoon then they will teach better and students will learn more so its really a win-win situation for everyone involved.

Some people might say that longer lunch breaks would mean less time for actual learning but I disagree because I think the quality of learning is more important than the quantity of learning. If students come back from lunch feeling refreshed and ready to learn then they will probably learn more in a shorter amount of time than if they came back from a rushed lunch feeling stressed and hungry. So in conclusion I believe that longer lunch breaks would be beneficial for students and teachers alike and schools should seriously consider making this change.

Have you ever wondered what makes a community truly thrive? The answer may surprise you: it starts with local libraries. In today's fast-paced world, the role of public libraries has never been more important, and communities everywhere should invest more in these vital institutions.

First and foremost, libraries play an essential role in education. They provide a wealth of resources that benefit learners of all ages. By offering access to books and other materials, libraries help people grow and develop. Education is the foundation of a strong society, and libraries support this foundation in meaningful ways. When communities invest in libraries, they invest in the future.

Additionally, libraries serve as important community gathering spaces. People from all walks of life come together in libraries, creating connections and building relationships. In an increasingly divided world, these spaces of unity are more valuable than ever. Libraries bring people together and help foster a sense of belonging that strengthens the social fabric.

Furthermore, libraries promote equality and access. Not everyone has the same resources at home, and libraries help level the playing field. By providing free access to information and technology, libraries ensure that everyone has the opportunity to succeed. This commitment to fairness and equal opportunity is something that benefits society as a whole.

In conclusion, public libraries are cornerstones of strong communities. Through their contributions to education, community building, and equal access, they make our towns and cities better places to live. It is imperative that we continue to support and invest in these remarkable institutions for the benefit of current and future generations. The time to act is now.

The drinking age in the United States should remain at 21. While there are several arguments on both sides of this debate, and reasonable people may disagree about the best approach, one particular piece of evidence is decisive.

Some argue that 18-year-olds are legal adults who can vote and serve in the military, so they should be allowed to drink. Others point to European countries where lower drinking ages have not caused societal collapse. These are understandable positions, but they miss the central issue.

The National Highway Traffic Safety Administration estimates that the National Minimum Drinking Age Act of 1984 has prevented approximately 31,959 traffic fatalities since its implementation. When the drinking age was raised from 18 to 21, alcohol-related traffic deaths among 18-to-20-year-olds dropped by 13% immediately and continued declining. This is not a correlation buried in noisy data; it is a clear, attributable effect of a specific policy change, tracked over four decades across all fifty states. The developing prefrontal cortex of young adults, responsible for impulse control and risk assessment, does not fully mature until approximately age 25, meaning 18-to-20-year-olds are neurologically less equipped to moderate alcohol's disinhibiting effects while making high-stakes decisions like driving.

Some say adults should have freedom even if it is risky. But we already restrict freedoms by age when the evidence warrants it: rental car companies charge more to under-25 drivers for actuarial reasons, and no one calls this tyranny. The question is not ideology but evidence, and the evidence here is unusually clean.

I think there is also something to be said about the cultural aspects of drinking and how different societies handle alcohol education, which is an interesting topic that would take a long time to fully explore and there are many perspectives worth considering.

In any case, there are many factors to consider and the issue is quite complex when you really think about all the different angles and stakeholders involved.

Many people argue that social media is beneficial for teenagers, but a careful examination of their claims reveals fundamental flaws in every major argument they present.

Proponents claim social media helps teens maintain friendships, but this ignores what Dunbar's research on social network size actually shows: humans can maintain roughly 150 meaningful relationships regardless of technology. Social media does not expand genuine social capacity; it merely creates an illusion of connection through superficial interactions like likes and brief comments. The MIT Media Lab's 2020 study found that teens who replaced in-person interaction with social media contact reported feeling lonelier, not more connected.

The argument that social media provides educational content is equally flawed. While platforms do host educational material, the algorithmic feed structure means informative content competes directly with sensationalised entertainment optimised for engagement. Research from the University of Amsterdam demonstrated that the average teen encounters 47 entertainment posts for every educational one. The claim that teens will self-select educational content contradicts everything we know about variable-ratio reinforcement schedules, which social media platforms deliberately exploit.

Defenders also assert that social media gives teens a creative outlet. However, the constraints of platform formats actively discourage genuine creativity. Instagram rewards conformity to trending aesthetics; TikTok's algorithm promotes imitation of viral formats. What is marketed as creative expression is more accurately described as template-following. Original content that deviates from platform norms is systematically suppressed by engagement-driven algorithms.

The mental health argument is perhaps the weakest defence. Advocates point to studies showing correlation between social media use and self-reported wellbeing, but these studies uniformly fail to control for the fact that teens with pre-existing strong social networks are both more likely to use social media and more likely to report positive mental health. When Haidt and Twenge controlled for this selection effect, the association between social media use and wellbeing reversed direction.

Finally, the free speech argument, that teens need platforms for self-expression, collapses under scrutiny. These are private platforms with content moderation policies that shape permissible expression. Teens are not exercising free speech; they are performing within the boundaries set by corporate content policies optimised for advertiser comfort.

Every pillar of the pro-social-media argument crumbles upon examination. The question of what teens should do instead remains open, but what they should not do is increasingly clear.
